content: Zoonotic diseases are infections transmitted from vertebrate animals to humans, accounting for approximately 60% of known human pathogens and 75% of emerging infectious diseases. Spillover occurs when a pathogen crosses the species barrier through: direct contact (bites, scratches), indirect contact (contaminated environments), vector-borne transmission (mosquitoes, ticks), or foodborne routes (undercooked meat, unpasteurized dairy). Drivers of spillover include: land use changes (deforestation, urbanization), wildlife trade, agricultural intensification, climate change altering vector ranges, and increased human-animal contact. Examples: HIV (primates), SARS-CoV-2 (likely bats via intermediate host), Ebola (bats), avian influenza (poultry).
source: WHO Zoonoses guidelines, CDC One Health framework
